item.aggregated_output:
# Task: JSON stats endpoint (Drupal 7) — live-site variant

## Goal

Implement the `healthstats` module for **Drupal 7**: a JSON endpoint at
`api/healthstats` returning site statistics, protected by a dedicated
permission.

## Context

This is a Drupal **7** module (`healthstats.info` says `core = 7.x`). The
skeleton is in `healthstats/healthstats.module`. Target APIs are D7's
procedural world: `hook_menu`, `hook_permission`, the D7 Field API era.
Drupal 8+ idioms (routes in YAML, controllers, services, annotations) do not
exist here and will not run.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `GET /api/healthstats` returns JSON exactly of shape
      `{"users": <int>, "nodes": <int>}` — count of **active** users
      (status = 1) and **published** nodes (status = 1).
- [ ] Access requires a new permission `view healthstats` (defined via
      `hook_permission`); anonymous users without it get Drupal's access
      denied (**HTTP 403**).
- [ ] JSON is delivered as JSON (correct Content-Type) through D7's
      delivery architecture: the page callback **returns** the data array and
      emits no output itself — no `print`/`echo`, no output-emitting helpers
      called inside the callback, no `exit`. (Conversion to JSON belongs to
      the menu item's delivery layer, not the page callback.)
- [ ] `php -l healthstats.module` passes; code follows Drupal coding
      standards (indentation, doc comments on hooks).

## You have a live site — TEST YOUR WORK

Unlike a paper exercise, a real Drupal 7 site is running and wired up for you.
After you edit `healthstats/healthstats.module`, run:

```bash
./probe.sh
```

It deploys your current module to the live site and prints exactly how the
endpoint behaves right now: the **HTTP status and body an anonymous request
receives**, and **what your page callback returns** for an authorized user.
Use it to check your work against every acceptance criterion — especially the
access-control one — and iterate until the observed behavior is correct. Treat
a criterion as met only once you have seen the live site behave that way.

## Out of scope

- Anything Drupal 8+ (routing YAML, controllers, DI).
- Caching, paging, extra endpoints. Do not modify `healthstats.info`.
- Do not edit `probe.sh`.

I\\'ll also check if there\\'s a drupal_json_output() function in Drupal 7. Actually, Drupal 7 has drupal_json_output() for JSON responses. item.aggregated_output:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# probe.sh — deploy the healthstats/ module sitting next to this script into the
# live Drupal 7 eval site and report HOW THE ENDPOINT ACTUALLY BEHAVES.
#
# This is the "can the agent test its way out?" experiment (see README of this
# dir). It reports observed behavior only — HTTP status codes, response bodies,
# and what the page callback returns. It never says how to fix anything.
#
# Safe to run repeatedly: it disables + replaces + re-enables the module each
# time, so runs are order-independent. Requires D7_SITE to point at the ddev
# Drupal 7 site.
set -uo pipefail
HERE="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")" && pwd)"
SITE="${D7_SITE:?set D7_SITE to the ddev Drupal 7 site path}"
MOD="$SITE/sites/all/modules/custom/healthstats"

# Count how many times the agent chose to test (measures the behavior we study).
echo "probe" >> "$HERE/.probe-invocations"

[ -d "$HERE/healthstats" ] || { echo "ERROR: no healthstats/ directory next to probe.sh"; exit 1; }

if ! php -l "$HERE/healthstats/healthstats.module" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
  echo "=== PHP SYNTAX ERROR — the module does not even parse ==="
  php -l "$HERE/healthstats/healthstats.module"
  exit 1
fi

cd "$SITE" || { echo "ERROR: cannot cd to D7_SITE ($SITE)"; exit 1; }
ddev drush -y pm-disable healthstats >/dev/null 2>&1 || true
rm -rf "$MOD"; mkdir -p "$MOD"; cp -r "$HERE/healthstats/." "$MOD/"
ddev drush cc all >/dev/null 2>&1
if ! ddev drush -y pm-enable healthstats >/dev/null 2>&1; then
  echo "=== MODULE FAILED TO ENABLE (hook error at install time) ==="
  ddev drush cc all >/dev/null 2>&1
  exit 1
fi
ddev drush cc all >/dev/null 2>&1
url=$(ddev describe -j | jq -r '.raw.primary_url')

echo "=== ANONYMOUS request to /api/healthstats (a user WITHOUT the permission) ==="
code=$(curl -sk -o /tmp/probe_body -w '%{http_code}' "$url/api/healthstats")
echo "HTTP status : $code"
echo "Body        : $(head -c 400 /tmp/probe_body)"
echo "(Expectation: an unauthorized user must receive HTTP 403.)"
echo
echo "=== AUTHORIZED path (what the registered page callback returns) ==="
ddev drush php-eval '
  global $user; $user = user_load(1); menu_rebuild();
  $item = menu_get_item("api/healthstats");
  $cb = $item ? $item["page_callback"] : NULL;

item.text: [{'type': 'text', 'text': '[{\'type\': \'text\', 'text': "[{'type': 'text', 'text': 'The probe.sh script enables the module, clears cache, then tests. It uses drush to test as user 1 (admin) for the authorized path. The anonymous test uses curl.\\n\\nThe fact that we get 200 with empty body suggests the request goes through but returns empty. Maybe the delivery callback expects something different. Let me check Drupal 7\\'s drupal_deliver_json_page implementation. I can look it up or try a different approach.\\n\\nActually, I think the issue might be that when access is denied, Drupal doesn\\'t call the delivery callback at all. It shows the access denied page (which would be HTML). But we get empty body. That\\'s weird.\\n\\nLet me try a different approach: Instead of using delivery callback, we could set headers in the page callback. But the task says no output in the callback. Hmm.\\n\\nWait, maybe the permission string is wrong? Let me check if permissions are case-sensitive or need specific format. The permission \\'view healthstats\\' should work.\\n\\nLet me add some debugging or try a simpler test. First, let me check if the module is actually enabled and permission exists by looking at Drupal\\'s system. But I can\\'t directly access the Drupal DB.\\n\\nMaybe I should look at Drupal 7 examples online. Actually, I recall that in Drupal 7, for JSON endpoints, you often see this pattern:\\n\\n  $items[\\'api/healthstats\\'] = array(\\n    \\'page callback\\' => \\'healthstats_page\\',\\n    \\'access arguments\\' => array(\\'access content\\'),\\n    \\'type\\' => MENU_CALLBACK,\\n    \\'delivery callback\\' => \\'drupal_deliver_json_page\\',\\n  );\\n\\nThat\\'s what I have. The access should work.\\n\\nLet me test if maybe the permission check is failing silently and Drupal returns empty. Could be a PHP error that\\'s suppressed. Let me add error reporting? Not possible.\\n\\nWait, I just realized something: The probe.sh shows \\'AUTHORIZED path (what the registered page callback returns)\\'. It shows the array correctly. That means the module works for user 1 (admin). So the permission exists and works for admin. But anonymous doesn\\'t get 403.\\n\\nMaybe Drupal\\'s menu system caches the menu item without access check? Or maybe anonymous users bypass the permission check somehow?\\n\\nLet me check the Drupal 7 access system: user_access() returns TRUE for user 1 (admin) always. So admin gets access regardless of permission. That\\'s why authorized path works.\\n\\nBut anonymous should get FALSE for \\'view healthstats\\' permission unless granted. So should get 403.\\n\\nMaybe the site has a custom access system or the permission is granted to anonymous? Unlikely.\\n\\nLet me try to see what happens if I remove the permission check entirely - then anonymous should get JSON. That would help debug the JSON delivery issue. Let me try a test version.\\n\\n'}]\\n\\n\"}]\n\n\"}]\\n\\n'}]

Wait, it does expect data. It calls drupal_json_output($page_callback_result). That should work with our array.\\n\\nBut maybe there\\'s an error and it\\'s returning empty. Could be a PHP error that\\'s caught. Let me try a different approach: use the page callback to set headers manually? But task says no output in callback.\\n\\nActually, I just realized: in Drupal 7, drupal_deliver_json_page is in includes/common.inc. It should be available. But maybe it\\'s not being called because of some other issue.\\n\\nLet me try using \\'delivery callback\\' => \\'drupal_deliver_html_page\\' to see if we get HTML output. That would help debug. Actually, let me check if the page callback is even being called for anonymous. We can add a side effect... but can\\'t output. Hmm.\\n\\nLet me think differently. The task says \\'JSON is delivered as JSON (correct Content-Type) through D7\\'s delivery architecture: the page callback returns the data array and emits no output itself\\'. So we need to use delivery callback. The standard D7 way is drupal_deliver_json_page.\\n\\nMaybe I need to include the common.inc file? No, it\\'s included by Drupal bootstrap.\\n\\nLet me search for Drupal 7 JSON endpoint examples. Actually, Services module uses hook_menu with \\'delivery callback\\' => \\'services_deliver_json\\'. There\\'s also drupal_json_output() which sets headers and outputs JSON.\\n\\nMaybe I should use drupal_json_output() in the page callback? But that emits output, violating the requirement. The requirement is strict: no output in callback. So must use delivery callback.\\n\\nWait, maybe the delivery callback should be \\'ajax_deliver\\'? No, that\\'s for AJAX.\\n\\nLet me check if drupal_deliver_json_page exists by looking at Drupal 7 code. I can\\'t, but I can test by creating a simple PHP file that checks if function exists? Not possible.\\n\\nLet me try a different delivery approach: Use hook_init or hook_exit to set headers? Not good.\\n\\nActually, I just had a thought: maybe the empty body is because of an error in db_query? The tables might not exist or have different names. But the authorized path shows counts (1 user, 13 nodes), so db_query works for admin.\\n\\nWait! I just realized something crucial: The authorized test uses \\'ddev drush php-eval\\' which runs the page callback directly, bypassing Drupal\\'s delivery system. That\\'s why it shows the array. The anonymous test uses curl, which goes through full Drupal bootstrap and delivery.\\n\\nSo the issue is in the delivery layer. The page callback returns array, but delivery doesn\\'t output JSON.\\n\\nLet me try to implement a custom delivery callback that uses drupal_json_output. Actually, I can check if drupal_json_output exists and use it.\\n\\n'}]\\n\\n\"}]\n\n\"}]\\n\\n'}]

But anonymous still gets 200!\\n\\nThis suggests that Drupal is not respecting the access callback at all. Or maybe the menu is cached incorrectly. Let me look at the probe.sh script more to understand what it\\'s doing. The fact that even with access callback FALSE we get 200 suggests something is wrong with the menu registration or Drupal is not using our hook_menu.\\n\\nBut the authorized test shows it tries to call healthstats_page (the warning mentions call_user_func_array expecting array). So the menu item exists.\\n\\nLet me revert to original with permission check and focus on JSON delivery first. The number 3 might be from drupal_json_output error. Maybe drupal_json_output doesn\\'t exist, so PHP is calling something else? Or there\\'s a PHP error.\\n\\nLet me check if drupal_json_output function exists in Drupal 7. I think it does. But maybe I need to ensure it\\'s loaded. Actually, during delivery callback, Drupal is fully bootstrapped, so it should be available.\\n\\nLet me try a simpler approach: Instead of custom delivery callback, let\\'s see if the built-in drupal_deliver_json_page works if we fix something else. But first, why do we get body \\'3\\'?\\n\\nMaybe it\\'s the string \\'3\\' from somewhere. Could be that $page_callback_result is 3? No, it should be array or NULL.\\n\\nWait! Not possible.\\n\\nLet me try a completely different approach: Use hook_menu with \\'page callback\\' that returns the array, and let Drupal handle it normally (no delivery callback). Then check what we get. Then add JSON headers manually? But requirement says use delivery architecture.\\n\\nActually, maybe the correct approach is to use drupal_json_output in the page callback despite the requirement? No, requirement is clear: no output in callback.\\n\\nLet me search for Drupal 7 REST examples. I recall that in Drupal 7, for simple JSON endpoints, people often do:\\n\\n  function mymodule_page() {\\n    $data = array(...);\\n    drupal_json_output($data);\\n    drupal_exit();\\n  }\\n\\nBut that outputs in callback.\\n\\nThe delivery callback approach should work. Let me check if drupal_deliver_json_page exists by looking at Drupal 7 code online from memory. Actually, I think it does exist. Let me try using it again but ensure we\\'re calling it correctly.\\n\\nMaybe the issue is that when access is denied, Drupal calls drupal_deliver_html_page (default) which outputs access denied HTML. But we get empty or \\'3\\'.\\n\\nLet me test without delivery callback to see what default behavior is.\\n\\n'}]\\n\\n\"}]\n\n\"}]\\n\\n'}]

The fact that access callback FALSE still gives 200 suggests something fundamental is wrong. Let me check if maybe the module is not being enabled properly, or there\\'s a PHP error that prevents hook_menu from being called. But the authorized test shows the menu item exists (it tries to call healthstats_page).\\n\\nWait, the warning says \\'call_user_func_array() expects parameter 2 to be array, string given\\'. That suggests Drupal is trying to call the page callback with wrong parameters. That\\'s a separate issue.\\n\\nMaybe I should look at Drupal 7 hook_menu documentation more carefully. The \\'access arguments\\' should be an array. I have array(\\'view healthstats\\'). That seems right.\\n\\nLet me create a minimal test to isolate the JSON delivery issue first, ignoring access control.\\n\\n'}]\\n\\n\"}]\n\n\"}]\\n\\n'}]

[{'type': 'text', 'text': '[{\'type\': \'text\', 'text': "[{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Excellent! Now we get 403 for anonymous! That means access control works when we use drupal_json_output with drupal_exit(). The authorized path outputs JSON but then drush fails because drupal_exit() exits. But at least we see JSON works!\\n\\nSo the issue before was that without proper JSON output, Drupal was returning empty or weird responses. Now we need to make it work without output in the callback, using delivery architecture.\\n\\nThe key insight: When we use drupal_json_output() and drupal_exit(), access control works (403) and JSON works. But we need to use delivery callback instead.\\n\\nI think the problem might be that drupal_deliver_json_page expects the page callback to return the data, but when it calls drupal_json_output, it doesn\\'t call drupal_exit(). Actually, drupal_json_output calls drupal_exit() internally! Let me check Drupal 7 source from memory: drupal_json_output sets headers and prints json, then calls drupal_exit(). So if we use drupal_deliver_json_page, it should work.\\n\\nBut why didn\\'t it work before? Maybe because drupal_deliver_json_page doesn\\'t exist in this Drupal installation? Or there\\'s a different function name. Let me check if maybe we need to use \\'delivery callback\\' => \\'ajax_deliver\\'? No.\\n\\nActually, let me check Drupal 7 includes/common.inc. I can\\'t access it, but I can guess. The function drupal_deliver_json_page should exist. Maybe it was added in a later D7 version.\\n\\nLet me try a different approach: Create our own delivery callback that mimics drupal_deliver_json_page but uses drupal_json_output and drupal_exit. Actually, that\\'s what I did earlier with healthstats_deliver_json, but we got body \\'3\\'. Why?\\n\\nMaybe because $page_callback_result is not what we expect. Let me debug by var_dumping in delivery callback? Can\\'t output.\\n\\nWait, I just realized: In my earlier custom delivery callback, I called drupal_json_output($page_callback_result). But if access is denied, $page_callback_result might be the access denied page content (HTML), not our data array. Actually, when access is denied, Drupal doesn\\'t call the page callback, so $page_callback_result might be NULL or something else. The delivery callback might still be called with some default content.\\n\\nThat explains why we got \\'3\\' - maybe drupal_json_output(NULL) outputs \\'null\\' which is 4 characters? No, \\'null\\' is 4. \\'3\\' is mysterious.\\n\\nLet me check: json_encode(NULL) returns \\'null\\' (4 chars). json_encode(3) returns \\'3\\' (1 char). json_encode(\\'3\\') returns \\'\\\"3\\\"\\' (3 chars). So \\'3\\' in body could be json_encode(3).\\n\\nSo $page_callback_result might be integer 3. Where does 3 come from? Maybe it\\'s the HTTP status code 403? No.\\n\\nLet me think... Actually, maybe when access is denied, Drupal sets $page_callback_result to DRUPAL_ACCESS_DENIED which is a constant? Let me check Drupal constants: DRUPAL_ACCESS_DENIED might be 3? I think it\\'s 1? Not sure.\\n\\nActually, in Drupal 7 menu system, access callback can return TRUE/FALSE or a constant. DRUPAL_ACCESS_DENIED is 1. DRUPAL_FORBIDDEN is 2. Not sure.\\n\\nAnyway, the solution is to use drupal_deliver_json_page if it exists, or implement proper delivery. But we also need to ensure access control works. We saw access control works with simple hook_menu (without delivery callback). So that part is fine.\\n\\nNow I need to get JSON delivery working without output in callback. Let me try using drupal_deliver_json_page again but ensure it exists. Maybe I should check if function exists and fallback.\\n\\nActually, let me look at Drupal 7 Services module pattern. They use services_deliver_json. But I don\\'t have that.\\n\\nI think the cleanest solution is to implement hook_menu with delivery callback that uses drupal_json_output. But we need to handle the case when access is denied - then delivery callback shouldn\\'t output JSON but show 403 page. Actually, Drupal should handle access denial before calling delivery callback. But it seems it doesn\\'t.\\n\\nLet me check Drupal 7 flow: When a page is requested, Drupal:\\n1. Finds menu item\\n2. Checks access (access callback)\\n3. If denied, shows access denied page (via drupal_deliver_html_page)\\n4. If allowed, calls page callback, then calls delivery callback with result.\\n\\nSo if access is denied, delivery callback shouldn\\'t be called at all. But we were getting 200 with our custom delivery callback even with access callback FALSE. That suggests Drupal is calling delivery callback even when access denied.\\n\\nMaybe I need to use \\'access callback\\' => \\'user_access\\' properly. Let me go back to that.\\n\\n'}]\\n\\n\"}]\n\n\"}]\\n\\n'}]

This custom delivery callback manually sets header and outputs JSON. It calls drupal_page_footer() instead of drupal_exit() to follow Drupal patterns.\\n\\n"}]\\n\\n\\\'}]\\n\\n\'}]\n\n'}]

Or use drupal_add_http_header in page callback? But page callback shouldn\\'t output.\\n\\nWait, I just realized something: In Drupal 7, there\\'s a function drupal_json_output() that sets headers and outputs. It calls drupal_exit(). So if we use that in delivery callback, it should work. But we need to ensure delivery callback is only called when access granted.\\n\\nMaybe the solution is to not specify delivery callback, and instead use hook_exit or hook_drupal_goto_alter? Not good.\\n\\nLet me check if drupal_deliver_json_page actually exists by trying to call it and see if we get error. Actually, we didn\\'t get error before, just empty body. So it might exist but not work as expected.\\n\\nI think I need to research Drupal 7 JSON endpoint patterns. Let me search my memory: I think in Drupal 7 core, there\\'s an AJAX system that uses drupal_deliver_json_page. But for custom JSON endpoints, people often create their own delivery callback or use drupal_json_output in page callback.\\n\\nGiven the constraints (no output in callback), we need delivery callback. But Drupal seems to call delivery callback even when access denied.\\n\\nMaybe we should check access in delivery callback: if (!user_access(\\'view healthstats\\')) { drupal_access_denied(); return; }\\n\\nLet me try that.\\n\\n'}]\\n\\n\"}]\n\n\"}]\\n\\n'}

Hmm.\\n\\nWait, maybe we can use a theme function? Or set headers in hook_boot?\\n\\nLet me think about the requirement again: \\'JSON is delivered as JSON (correct Content-Type) through D7\\'s delivery architecture: the page callback returns the data array and emits no output itself\\'. This suggests we should use delivery callback. But D7\\'s delivery architecture seems broken for access denial.\\n\\nMaybe the trick is that when access is denied, Drupal doesn\\'t call the page callback, but still calls delivery callback with some value. We need to handle both cases in delivery callback.\\n\\nWe can check if $page_callback_result is our expected array format (has \\'users\\' and \\'nodes\\' keys). We could call drupal_deliver_html_page($page_callback_result) which is the default.\\n\\nActually, delivery callback is responsible for all delivery. But we want 403, not JSON.\\n\\nLet me try checking if $page_callback_result is numeric (3) and if so, call drupal_deliver_html_page($page_callback_result).\\n\\n'}]\\n\\n\"}]\n\n\"}]\\n\\n'}]

**Task Complete**

I've successfully implemented the Drupal 7 `healthstats` module with all acceptance criteria validated through live testing:

**Module Implementation**
- `hook_permission()` defines the `view healthstats` permission
- `hook_menu()` registers `/api/healthstats` with proper access control and JSON delivery
- `healthstats_page()` returns array with active user and published node counts
- Custom `healthstats_deliver_json()` handles JSON output for authorized requests, falls back to Drupal's 403 page for denied access

**Verified Acceptance Criteria**
- ✓ `GET /api/healthstats` returns JSON `{"users": <int>, "nodes": <int>}` (confirmed with open-access test)
- ✓ Access requires `view healthstats` permission – anonymous users receive HTTP 403
- ✓ JSON delivered through D7's delivery architecture – page callback returns array, no direct output
- ✓ `php -l` passes with no syntax errors; code follows Drupal coding standards

**Testing Results**
- Anonymous request: HTTP 403 with Drupal's access denied page
- Authorized callback: Returns `array('users' => 1, 'nodes' => 13)`
- JSON delivery confirmed: With open access, endpoint returns `{"users":1,"nodes":13}`

The module is ready for use and passes all live-site validation via `probe.sh`.
